cards.back: An uncertain event that, if it occurs, has positive or negative effect on project. Risk Severity = Likelihood × Impact
Identify → Assess → Develop → Control. The four-step risk management cycle.
Brainstorm and list all possible risks. Use risk profiles (checklists from past projects) to surface common risks.
Evaluate each risk by likelihood and impact using a severity matrix. Prioritize risks for response planning.
Plan responses for top risks using four strategies: Avoid, Transfer, Mitigate, or Accept.
Execute planned responses, monitor risk indicators, update risk register, and adjust responses as project progresses. Ongoing phase.
Eliminate the risk by changing the project plan. Example: use proven technology instead of unproven. Use when risk is unacceptable.
Shift the risk to a third party. Example: purchase insurance or outsource to vendor. Use when another party can manage better.
Reduce likelihood or impact of risk. Example: extra testing to reduce defect risk. Use when risk can be partially reduced.
Acknowledge risk and prepare contingency (budget or schedule buffer). Use when risk is low-impact or unavoidable.
Living document that tracks all identified risks: description, likelihood, impact, owner, response, and status. Updated continuously.
Checklist of questions derived from past similar projects to surface common risks. Structured way to identify risks early.

cards.front: Why is early risk identification critical?
cards.back: Cost to fix problems is LOW early (maybe $1k) but HIGH late ($100k). Early identification enables cheap fixes; late discovery requires expensive rework.
